Document [18] (Title: Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever (Rickettsia rickettsii) -- Treatment / Management) Doxycycline is the drug of choice for the treatment of Rocky Mountain spotted fever, including in children. Defervescence typically occurs within three days of starting appropriate antibiotic therapy, although other symptoms may be slower to resolve. The treatment course is usually seven to ten days, or for at least three days after defervescence. It is important to note than in severe rickettsial disease, such as Rocky Mountain spotted fever, mortality rates are as high as 20% to 30% without prompt antibiotic treatment. Thus, treatment should not delay while awaiting confirmatory laboratory testing in a patient with a suspected rickettsial infection; this is particularly important in children, who are at high risk of morbidity and mortality but may experience delays in treatment due to concerns with doxycycline use in children younger than age 8. The risk of dental staining in the treatment of Rocky Mountain spotted fever is very low, as dental staining is a cumulative dose effect, and treatment for Rocky Mountain spotted fever is typically seven to ten days. Thus, concerns regarding tooth staining should not delay treatment in suspected Rocky Mountain spotted fever. [11] [12] [13] [14]

[25] Erythromycin is prescribed at a dose of 500 mg 4 times a day for a 2-week duration. Penicillin G is administered intramuscularly, with a dosage of 300,000 units every 12 hours for patients weighing 10 kg or less and 600,000 units every 12 hours for those weighing more than 10 kg. Once the patient can take oral medication, oral penicillin V can be initiated at a dose of 250 mg 4 times daily for 2 weeks. Early initiation of antibiotics is crucial to eliminate the organism promptly, limiting toxin release, expediting patient recovery, and preventing infection spread to close contacts. In cases of antibiotic resistance, linezolid or vancomycin may be considered.

Document [0] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 377) Minocycline, doxycycline, tetracycline hydrochloride, and tigecycline are all classified as tetracycline antibiotics, a broad-spectrum antimicrobial class used to treat a variety of bacterial infections. Minocycline is available in several oral dosage forms, including a 45 mg extended‑release capsule and tablet, a 50 mg oral capsule and tablet, and a 75 mg oral tablet, each of which belongs to the tetracycline antibiotic family. Doxycycline is marketed in multiple formulations: a 100 mg injection, a 100 mg oral capsule, delayed‑release tablets in 50 mg, 150 mg, and 200 mg strengths, and a 150 mg oral capsule of doxycycline monohydrate, all of which are tetracycline antibiotics. Additionally, tetracycline hydrochloride 250 mg oral capsule and tigecycline 50 mg injection are also members of the tetracycline antibiotic class. Recognizing that these diverse dosage forms and strengths share a common pharmacologic class is essential for clinicians when selecting appropriate therapy, managing potential class‑related adverse effects, and ensuring consistent antimicrobial stewardship across different patient populations.
